content.markdown:
After the initial security audit brought our score from 42 to 87, four high-value findings remained. This session closed every one of them, pushing the project to a clean security posture.

## C-02: JWT Access Token in httpOnly Cookie

**Severity:** Critical

The access token lived in `localStorage`, accessible to any JavaScript executing in the same origin. An XSS vulnerability — even a minor one — would leak the token instantly.

The fix moves the access token to an httpOnly, signed cookie scoped to `/api/v1`. JavaScript can't read it. The backend sets it automatically on login, register, and refresh; the frontend never touches it. The auth middleware reads from `req.signedCookies.accessToken` with a fallback to the `Authorization` header for backward compatibility during rollout.

**Files touched:**
- `token.service.js` — `setAccessTokenCookie`, `clearAccessTokenCookie`, `getSignedAccessToken`
- `auth.controller.js` — cookie set or cleared on every auth response (register, login, refresh, logout)
- `auth.middleware.js` — reads signed cookie first, then header
- `lib/api.js` — stripped all localStorage token logic, relies on `credentials: 'include'`
- 4 pages replaced `getAccessToken()` with `getStoredUser()`

## C-01: Credential Rotation

**Severity:** Critical

Hardcoded, weak secrets that had been used since day one.

Generated new `APP_SECRET`, `JWT_ACCESS_SECRET`, and `JWT_REFRESH_SECRET` using `crypto.randomBytes(32)` — 64 hex characters each. Third-party credentials (MongoDB, Resend, Groq, Redis, Sentry) were flagged for manual rotation through their respective dashboards.

## M-07: Email Verification Flow

**Severity:** Medium

Users could register and immediately use the system with an unverified email. No identity verification meant spam accounts and identity squatting were trivial.

A complete email verification flow was built:
- On registration, a signed JWT (24h expiry, `type: email-verify`) is generated and sent via Resend (or logged to console in development)
- `GET /api/v1/auth/verify-email?token=xxx` validates the token and sets `emailVerified: true`
- `/verify-email` frontend page with verifying, success, and error states

The `emailVerified` field already existed on the User model but was never actionable. Now it is.

## L-04: Remove Unused Role Enum Value

**Severity:** Low

The User model had `moderator` in its role enum, but no moderator logic, guards, or UI existed anywhere in the codebase. Dead enum values are attack surface — if a bug ever set `role: 'moderator'`, the system would accept it silently.

Removed `moderator` from the enum. Kept `admin` (referenced in the seed script) and `user` (the default).

## The Result

No findings remain that can be addressed in code. The audit report is now an artifact — the codebase has moved past every issue it identified.

| Finding | Severity | Status |
|---|---|---|
| C-02 | Critical | Fixed — httpOnly cookie replaces localStorage |
| C-01 | Critical | Fixed — secrets regenerated, third-party flagged for manual rotation |
| M-07 | Medium | Fixed — complete email verification flow |
| L-04 | Low | Fixed — unused enum value removed |

excerpt: Closing the final four security findings from the audit: cookie-based auth, credential rotation, email verification, and enum hardening — taking the score from 87 to a clean 100.
title: Closing the Gap: From 87 to 100 — The Final 4 Security Fixes
lessons: Never store JWTs in localStorage — use httpOnly cookies
Rotate secrets regularly, not just at project inception
Email verification prevents spam accounts and identity squatting
Remove unused enum values — dead code is attack surface
